title-group.article-title: The post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae: manifestations, mechanisms and treatment strategies

abstract.p: Recent studies have increasingly demonstrated that coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) patients may develop long-term sequelae of varying severity, collectively referred to as long COVID or post-COVID-19 condition. Pulmonary sequelae are particularly common, which significantly impair patients’ quality of life. The mechanisms underlying post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae are complex and multifactorial, and their management is still at an exploratory stage. This review explores the manifestations, underlying mechanisms, and potential treatment approaches for post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae. Fatigue, dyspnea, myalgia, and sleep disturbances are the most commonly reported symptoms following COVID-19 infection, while anxiety and depression are also prevalent. Respiratory symptoms include dyspnoea, persistent cough, hypoxia, and reduced exercise capacity. Impaired lung diffusion capacity is the most frequently observed pulmonary function abnormality, and residual abnormalities on chest computed tomography (CT) commonly include ground-glass opacities (GGO) and fibrotic-like changes. Air trapping is also an important CT finding and has been reported to associated with impaired lung diffusion function. The potential mechanisms may include pulmonary fibrosis, chronic inflammation, immune dysregulation, coagulation abnormalities and thrombosis, and persistent viral infection. Current treatment strategies encompass vaccination, pulmonary rehabilitation, and pharmacological interventions such as antifibrotic, anti-inflammatory, and anticoagulant therapies. A comprehensive understanding of the recovery trajectory and the mechanisms underlying post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae is crucial for improving patient outcomes.

award-group.funding-source: the National Natural Science Foundation of China

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has spread rapidly around the world since its outbreak in 2019, posing unprecedented challenges to national public health systems and the global economy. COVID-19 is caused by infection with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), primarily transmitted through the respiratory tract (1). The lungs are the primary target organ of SARS-CoV-2, with patients commonly presenting symptoms resembling those of respiratory tract infections, which may progress to severe pneumonia (2). According to the latest statistics from the World Health Organization (WHO), as of January 14, 2025, there have been over 700 million confirmed COVID-19 cases and over 7 million deaths worldwide (3).

SARS-CoV-2 belongs to the Coronaviridae family and, like Middle East Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERS-CoV) and SARS-CoV, is a human-infecting coronavirus (4). However, its viral dynamics differ from MERS-CoV and SARS-CoV (5). Compared to MERS and SARS, COVID-19 typically presents with milder clinical symptoms and lower mortality rates, but it spreads more rapidly within populations, leading to a higher number of infections and thus posing a greater risk to human health (6,7). According to the WHO (8), from January 2020 to November 2024, approximately 5% of confirmed COVID-19 cases required hospitalization, while about 6.9–24.5% of hospitalized patients required intensive care unit (ICU) care. The mortality rate among hospitalized cases has gradually decreased, from 25.3% in 2021 to 4.1% in 2024. The reduction in the severity and mortality of acute COVID-19 infections is associated with the diminished virulence of viral variants and the widespread vaccination (9-11). However, as survival rates increase and the number of COVID-19 recoveries rises, the number of patients affected by post-COVID-19 sequelae is also expected to rise.

Increasing evidence indicates that COVID-19 patients may experience a range of long-term sequelae affecting multiple organ systems (12-14). This condition is commonly termed long COVID or post-COVID-19 condition (15). There are many definitions of post-COVID-19 condition (Table 1), but there is currently no globally standardized and universally accepted definition. The principal differences in the definitions of long COVID focus on the onset timing and duration of symptoms. The gradual refinement of the definition of long COVID will help improve the accuracy of diagnosis and research.

tr.td: Persistence or development of new symptoms 3 months after the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection, with these symptoms lasting for at least 2 months, with no other explanation
Ongoing, relapsing, or new symptoms or conditions present 30 or more days after infection
Signs and symptoms that continue or develop after acute COVID‑19, which includes both ongoing symptomatic COVID‑19 (from 4 to 12 weeks) and post‑COVID‑19 syndrome (12 weeks or more)
Occur after SARS-CoV-2 infection, and persist for at least 3 months as a continuous, relapsing and remitting, or progressive disease state affecting one or more organ systems
An IACC that occurs following SARS-CoV-2 infection, persists for at least 3 months and involves symptoms affecting multiple organ systems

table-wrap-foot.p: CDC, The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; COVID-19, coronavirus disease 2019; IACC, infection-associated chronic condition; NASEM, The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine; NICE, The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; PASC, Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19; SARS-CoV-2, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2; WHO, World Health Organization.

sec.p: Among symptomatic COVID-19 patients, approximately 6.2% experience long COVID symptoms, with respiratory symptoms being one of the major symptom clusters, affecting 3.7% of patients and significantly impacting their quality of life (21). The pathogenesis of post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae is complex and may involve various physiological and pathological processes, including viral-induced cellular damage, immune system dysregulation, and coagulation abnormalities (22). Currently, treatment strategies for pulmonary sequelae remain largely in the research phase. Post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae require ongoing clinical attention and timely intervention. A comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms and progression of pulmonary sequelae is essential for developing effective interventions and promoting pulmonary rehabilitation (PR). This article reviews the manifestations, underlying mechanisms, and potential treatment strategies for post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae, offering scientific evidence to guide clinical practice and patient care.

sec.title: Clinical manifestations
sec.p: Post-COVID-19 sequelae affect multiple organ systems, leading to a wide range of physical, psychological, and cognitive symptoms (13). These symptoms may impair patients’ capacity to engage in physical and daily activities, as well as their ability to return to work. Studies indicate that approximately 10% to 13% of individuals, including both hospitalized and non-hospitalized patients, continue to experience persistent symptoms after recovering from acute COVID-19 or develop new symptoms beyond 4 weeks post-infection (23,24). Although the severity of acute infection is associated with COVID-19 sequelae (25,26), the majority of long COVID cases occur in non-hospitalized patients with mild acute illness, as this group constitutes the majority of total cases of SARS-CoV-2 infection (27).

Fatigue, dyspnea, myalgia, and sleep disturbances are the most commonly reported symptoms following COVID-19 infection (28-30). Anxiety and depression are also prevalent, with a follow-up study of hospitalized COVID-19 patients showing that 26% and 12% of patients experienced anxiety or depression 1 and 2 years after discharge, respectively (31,32). At the 2-year follow-up, the quality-of-life assessments of COVID-19 survivors remained lower compared to the control group of uninfected individuals (31,33). A prospective study indicated that while symptoms tend to improve over time, the rate of recovery in the late convalescent period (6–12 months) is significantly slower compared to the early convalescent period (0–3 months) (34).

Some individuals with post-COVID-19 symptoms are unable to return to normal work. A study investigating the return to work after COVID-19 found that 6.6% of hospitalized patients and 36% of ICU patients had not resumed work 6 months after discharge (35). A prospective cohort study revealed that 45% of previously employed patients had not returned to full-time work 1 year after hospital discharge, and 49% of patients self-reported difficulties with memory or concentration (36).

Regarding respiratory involvement, common symptoms include dyspnoea, coughing, hypoxia, and reduced exercise capacity, with dyspnoea being the most frequently reported respiratory sequela (37-39). Longitudinal studies indicated that approximately 40% to 45% of hospitalized COVID-19 patients continue to experience varying degrees of dyspnoea 1 year after discharge (40,41). According to the modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) dyspnoea scale, the most common score is 1 (dyspnoea when hurrying or walking up a slight hill), followed by a score of 2 (walking slower than people of the same age due to dyspnoea or needing to stop for breath when walking at one’s own pace) (40,41). Even after 2 years of follow-up, 44% of patients exhibited an mMRC score of 2 or higher (14). Risk factors for post-COVID-19 dyspnea include socioeconomic deprivation, a history of anxiety or depression, advanced age, female gender, ICU admission, and prolonged hospital stay (42-44). Understanding these risk factors can help target interventions for high-risk populations and provide deeper insights into the underlying mechanisms (44).

Compared to earlier variants, Omicron infections are associated with significantly reduced clinical symptoms and a lower risk of pneumonia. A retrospective study found that the severity of pneumonia in Omicron-infected individuals was approximately 71% of that in Delta-infected individuals, and the probability of ICU admission or in-hospital death was about 43% of that in Delta-infected individuals (45). Another study showed that the incidence of severe disease in Delta infection was 4.6 times higher than in Omicron infection (46). Additionally, the incidence of long COVID in Omicron cases was lower compared to Delta, with a reduction in risk ranging from 24% to 50% (47). However, compared to other common respiratory infections, patients infected with the Omicron variant are more likely to experience prolonged symptoms such as fatigue and dyspnoea (48). Therefore, long-term follow-up is essential to monitor and manage these symptoms. A study has demonstrated that the reinfection rate of the Omicron variant is significantly higher in long COVID patients than in those without long COVID, with an increased incidence of dyspnoea observed following reinfection (49). This highlights the importance of enhancing the management of long COVID patients and implementing effective strategies to prevent reinfection.

sec.title: Pulmonary function assessment
sec.p: Multiple studies have demonstrated that the most prevalent pulmonary function abnormality in patients recovering from COVID-19 is impaired lung diffusion capacity, specifically characterized by a diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO) % predicted <80% (50-52). Ventilatory dysfunction is less common compared to diffusion impairment. A 1-year follow-up study revealed that only 7% of hospitalized patients exhibited restrictive ventilatory dysfunction, 3.8% had obstructive ventilatory dysfunction, and 39.7% presented with impaired lung diffusion (40). Factors associated with impaired lung diffusion capacity at the 1-year follow-up include age, female sex, body mass index (BMI), and ferritin levels during hospitalization (53).

However, the recovery trends for lung diffusion capacity vary across studies. Some early follow-up studies (3–6 months post-discharge) have shown gradual improvement in pulmonary diffusion function (54,55). Yet, another study indicated that no significant improvement in diffusion impairment was observed between 6 and 12 months (32). Another 1-year follow-up study demonstrated that 53.8% of patients had diffusion impairment at 60 days post-discharge, which gradually improved over time, with rates of 46.8% at 6 months and 39.8% at 1 year (53). In a 3-year follow-up study of hospitalized COVID-19 patients, the proportion of patients with pulmonary diffusion dysfunction decreased from 49% at 6 months to 38% at 3 years, although still higher than in healthy controls (56). Another study showed a downward trend in DLCO, gradually decreasing from 6 months to 1 and 2 years, with total lung capacity (TLC) also showing a decreasing trend, regardless of whether the patients were in the moderate-to-severe or critically ill group (57). The ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) to forced vital capacity (FVC) remained relatively stable over 2 years, around 79% (57). The observed differences in recovery trends may be attributed to regional variations in follow-up and the heterogeneity of patient populations. Corticosteroid therapy and PR initiated within 6 weeks after discharge have been shown to enhance pulmonary function recovery (57,58). Further research is needed to elucidate the trajectory of respiratory function recovery and the factors influencing it.

sec.title: Radiological findings
sec.p: The early chest computed tomography (CT) findings in COVID-19 infection mainly include ground-glass opacities (GGO), consolidation, crazy paving patterns, and reticular patterns (59,60). The extent of lung involvement on chest CT peaks between 6 to 12 days after the onset of symptoms (61,62). However, residual lesions may persist on chest CT in some patients after discharge (62,63). Common residual abnormalities include GGO and fibrotic-like changes (traction bronchiectasis, parenchymal bands, and/or honeycombing), while consolidation tends to resolve gradually (64,65). A follow-up study found that the proportion of COVID-19 survivors with residual lung abnormalities declined from 46% at 6 months to 36% at 3 years, with non-fibrotic lesions gradually resolving, while fibrotic-like lesions remained largely stable (56). Patients who undergo invasive mechanical ventilation, are older, or have more severe disease during hospitalization are more likely to present with radiological abnormalities (40,66,67).

Some studies have proposed that fibrotic-like changes should be distinguished from fibrotic abnormalities, as COVID-19-induced fibrosis differs from chronic interstitial lung diseases (ILDs), underscoring the importance of differentiating acute inflammation-driven fibrotic-like abnormalities from chronic progressive fibrotic tissue proliferation (64,68). In a 6-month follow-up study, Han et al. reported that approximately 35% of patients with severe COVID-19 showed fibrotic-like changes on chest CT (67). A subsequent 1-year follow-up revealed a reduction in overall lesion scores, GGO, and reticular structures on CT, although fibrotic-like changes and traction bronchiectasis scores remained unchanged (68). Another long-term follow-up study reached similar conclusions (69,70). At the 6-month follow-up, 32% of patients discharged with a diagnosis of severe COVID-19 pneumonia exhibited fibrotic-like changes (69). Over the subsequent 2 years, non-fibrotic GGO significantly decreased, whereas fibrotic-like changes persisted, with a slight reduction in severity, but without developing new honeycombing or traction bronchiectasis (70). This suggests that residual abnormalities observed on CT may represent a reparative process of acute lung injury rather than progressive fibrosis and may not be irreversible during the follow-up period (70,71). Carvalho et al. observed that among patients presenting with fibrotic-like changes at the 1-year follow-up, 5% showed improvement by the second year, with the fibrotic-like lesions regressing to non-fibrotic lesions (14).

Additionally, early follow-up studies identified air trapping on chest CT in some long COVID patients with respiratory symptoms, suggesting the involvement of the small airways (72,73). At the 6-month follow-up, approximately one-third of hospitalized patients exhibited significant air trapping, which was associated with impaired lung diffusion function (74). Follow-up from the first to the second year showed an increase in the number of patients with small airway abnormalities, including mosaic attenuation and bronchial wall thickening, although CT scores did not demonstrate a significant change (14). This suggests that in some long COVID patients, small airway damage may represent a persistent and chronic process. Recently, histopathological evidence has further supported the involvement of small airways. Gagiannis et al. reported that, several months after mild COVID-19 infection, some patients exhibited marked T lymphocyte infiltration—particularly CD4+ T cells—around the small airways in lung tissue (75). Additionally, elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β were detected in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) samples, suggesting that the small airways may represent a key site of ongoing inflammation (75). The small airway abnormalities suggested by air trapping may affect the lung recovery process in patients. Therefore, incorporating the detection of air trapping and small airway abnormalities into the long-term monitoring of long COVID patients is essential for providing a more comprehensive evaluation of pulmonary recovery.

sec.title: Mechanisms
sec.p: The mechanisms underlying post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae are complex and multifactorial, potentially involving pulmonary fibrosis, chronic inflammation, immune dysregulation, coagulation abnormalities and thrombosis, and persistent viral infection.

sec.title: Pulmonary fibrosis
sec.p: One potential cause of long-term pulmonary symptoms in COVID-19 recovery patients is pulmonary fibrosis (Figure 1). Patients with suspected post-COVID-19 ILD are more likely to present with cough symptoms and abnormal pulmonary function (76,77). Fibrosis is typically understood as the formation of irreversible fibrotic “scars” resulting from dysregulation in the wound healing response, which is characterized by an excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix (ECM) components, such as collagen and fibronectin, at the site of tissue injury (78). SARS-CoV-2 can directly or indirectly damage alveolar epithelial cells by binding to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors on cell surfaces or through immune-mediated mechanisms (79,80). Furthermore, mechanical ventilation during hospitalization may cause airway damage (81,82). The injury leads to the accumulation of immune cells and the secretion of various cytokines, triggering an inflammatory response, activating fibroblasts, and promoting ECM synthesis to repair the damaged tissue (83). During severe viral infection, excessive immune cell reactions can lead to prolonged or exacerbated inflammation, thereby preventing effective control of the injury or further aggravating it (79,84,85). Severe alveolar injury caused by COVID-19 may persist even after viral clearance (86,87).

caption.p: The mechanisms of post-COVID-19 pulmonary fibrosis. Mechanical ventilation-induced airway injury, direct damage to type II alveolar cells caused by SARS-CoV-2 infection via the ACE2 receptor, and immune-mediated injury resulting from cytokine storms and immune cell infiltration, all contribute to the continuous activation of fibroblasts, leading to the excessive secretion of ECM and the development of pulmonary fibrosis. ACE2, angiotensin-converting enzyme 2; COVID-19, coronavirus disease 2019; ECM, extracellular matrix; SARS-CoV-2, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2.

sec.p: Kamp et al. found that COVID-19-related lung injury exhibits a time-dependent progression from inflammation to fibrosis (88). Analysis of lung tissues from patients who died of respiratory failure due to COVID-19 revealed that early deaths were characterized by predominant expression of inflammation-related genes, whereas late deaths showed significantly increased expression of fibrosis-associated genes, accompanied by histological features typical of fibrosis. In a surgical lung biopsy study of COVID-19 patients with persistent ILD findings during the recovery phase, approximately 50% of patients demonstrated a histological pattern consistent with usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP), while others exhibited features of acute lung injury (89). Notably, many patients with UIP-like changes had a pre-existing history of lung disease, suggesting that these pathological alterations are more likely to reflect the progression of underlying ILD following viral infection rather than fibrosis directly induced by SARS-CoV-2. Some researchers have proposed that COVID-19-associated fibrosis should not be classified as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) (90). Although some patients may exhibit “UIP-like” structural remodeling on imaging and histology, such changes are more likely to represent fibrosis secondary to post-infectious inflammation and tissue repair processes. In a study using transbronchial cryobiopsy in COVID-19 survivors with persistent symptoms and chest CT abnormalities, Ravaglia et al. identified three histological patterns: (I) a chronic fibrosing pattern, resembling UIP with architectural distortion and honeycombing; (II) an acute/subacute injury and repair pattern, including organizing pneumonia (OP), fibrosing nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP), and diffuse alveolar damage (DAD); and (III) a vascular-dominant pattern characterized by proliferation and dilation of capillaries and small venules (91). Among these, the first pattern likely reflects exacerbation of pre-existing ILD, with COVID-19 acting as a trigger or masking factor, rather than initiating de novo pulmonary fibrosis. Post-COVID-19 pulmonary fibrosis can present with diverse histopathological patterns, each potentially associated with distinct pathogenic mechanisms. Therefore, distinguishing newly developed fibrotic changes from the progression of pre-existing ILD is crucial for personalized treatment planning and prognostic evaluation. Ravaglia et al. emphasized that progression of pre-existing ILD typically manifests as persistent fibrotic abnormalities on imaging with classic UIP-like features, whereas post-COVID-19 fibrotic changes may show partial resolution over time (91). This observation is consistent with findings from Lazar et al., who reported a significant overall decline in fibrosis severity during a 2-year follow-up of patients with severe COVID-19 (92).

Furthermore, an in vitro study demonstrated that neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs), in a simulated COVID-19 infection environment, can induce epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in lung epithelial cells, which is a potential mechanism of pulmonary fibrosis (93). A comparison of blood and upper respiratory samples from patients with abnormal lung findings 3 to 6 months after recovery and those with complete recovery revealed that the former had higher levels of NET-associated proteins (94). This indicates a relationship between post-COVID-19 pulmonary fibrosis and neutrophil-related inflammatory mechanisms, suggesting potential therapeutic targets.

sec.title: Chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation
sec.p: Chronic inflammation induced by SARS-CoV-2 infection is another potential cause of post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae. Multiple studies have reported persistent abnormalities in the expression of inflammation-related cytokines and genes in patients after COVID-19 infection. In a 20-month longitudinal follow-up of patients with long COVID, Bae et al. found that levels of inflammatory cytokines such as IFN-α2, IFN-γ, and TNF-α remained elevated compared to healthy controls, suggesting a sustained chronic inflammatory response (95). Similarly, Fineschi et al. observed continued upregulation of inflammation-related genes such as JAK3 and STAT1/3 in some long COVID patients even 28 months after infection, despite the absence of detectable viral remnants, indicating that the inflammation was not driven by persistent viral infection (96). Additionally, another study has shown that, compared with fully recovered COVID-19 patients, long COVID patients exhibit significant upregulation of genes such as ICOS and S1PR1, which are associated with T cell activation and inflammatory responses (97). Chemokines CCL3 and CCL19 were markedly elevated in patients with impaired lung function and were correlated with decreased DLCO, suggesting their potential role as biomarkers of chronic pulmonary inflammation (98). Different clinical manifestations of long COVID may involve distinct inflammatory pathways. For instance, cardiopulmonary symptoms have been linked to elevated levels of inflammatory markers such as IL-1R2, MATN2, and COLEC12, offering potential targets for therapeutic intervention (99).

Immune dysregulation is also believed to play a key role in the development of post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae. In COVID-19 convalescent patients, significant increases in γ/δ T cells, B cells, and CD8+ T cells have been observed in BAL samples, which is closely associated with impaired lung function and radiographic abnormalities (100). BAL samples from patients with persistent respiratory symptoms also showed marked increases in T cells, B cells, and macrophages, indicating ongoing immune dysregulation in the respiratory tract after COVID-19 (101). Severe SARS-CoV-2 infection may induce the generation of cross-reactive antibodies by B cells that recognize both viral antigens and host components, thereby triggering autoimmune responses (102). In some patients with post-COVID-19 conditions, abnormal B cell activation and the formation of autoantibodies have been observed, with autoantibody titers remaining elevated and even new autoantibody types emerging as long as 1 year after infection (103). However, there is evidence suggesting that immune dysregulation may also be transient. Phetsouphanh et al. found that some long COVID patients exhibited marked immune activation—elevated antibody levels and T cell activation markers—within 3 to 8 months post-infection. By 2 years, these alterations had largely resolved, with most patients experiencing significant improvement in quality of life (104).

Notably, chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation often coexist. In the study by Woodruff et al., 55% of patients with elevated plasma inflammatory markers were positive for autoantibodies, a proportion significantly higher than in patients with normal inflammation levels (103). This suggests a bidirectional relationship, where chronic inflammation may promote autoantibody production, and these autoantibodies may further exacerbate inflammation.

sec.title: Coagulation abnormalities and thrombosis
sec.p: Research identified the presence of microthrombi in the plasma of long COVID patients, with significantly elevated levels of α2-antiplasmin, which impair the dissolution of these microthrombi (105). A study on viral pneumonia patients requiring extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) support showed that COVID-19 patients exhibited a 12-fold higher risk of thrombosis compared to patients with other viral pneumonias (106). Coagulation abnormalities and thrombosis are potential causes of persistent symptoms such as fatigue and shortness of breath. The possible mechanisms for thrombosis include endothelial dysfunction, a hyperinflammatory immune response, and a hypercoagulable state (107). SARS-CoV-2 can damage endothelial cells either directly through infection or indirectly by activating immune cells that release pro-inflammatory cytokines, thereby reducing the anti-thrombotic activity of endothelial cells (108-110). The spike protein of the virus can bind to fibrinogen, forming pro-inflammatory thrombi that are resistant to plasmin, leading to systemic thrombosis and inflammation (111). A study also demonstrated that blood samples from long COVID patients exhibit significant increases in markers of tissue damage, platelet activation, and thrombotic inflammation (112). Red blood cell (RBC) abnormalities may play a critical role in microvascular flow disturbances observed in patients with long COVID. Studies have shown that convalescent COVID-19 patients may exhibit structural damage to the RBC membrane and morphological alterations, along with reduced deformability and increased aggregation (113-115). These abnormalities impair microvascular circulation and may be associated with persistent post-COVID-19 symptoms. Hemorheological alterations in RBCs can indirectly promote thrombosis by inducing local blood flow slowing, tissue hypoxia, and endothelial injury. Although elevated levels of 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) have been observed in COVID-19 patients, potentially reflecting a compensatory response to hypoxia, such compensation may be insufficient to alleviate oxygen deficiency under conditions of RBC membrane damage and metabolic dysregulation and may instead aggravate RBC injury (116-118). While hypercoagulability is typically associated with hospitalized and severe COVID-19 cases, research indicates that even non-hospitalized patients with mild or asymptomatic infections exhibit a higher risk of thrombosis compared to uninfected controls (119). Therefore, in patients with mild symptoms during the acute phase of infection but persistent symptoms during recovery, the formation of microthrombi should be considered as a potential contributing factor.

sec.title: Persistent viral infection
sec.p: SARS-CoV-2 infection can lead to persistent infection in some patients, representing another significant concern. Generally, the average duration of viral shedding after infection is 17 to 30 days (5,120,121), although some patients remain positive for polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for up to 400 days post-infection (122,123). This phenomenon is more prevalent in immunocompromised patients, including those undergoing immunosuppressive therapy or those with lymphoma (124-126). Additionally, elevated IL-6 levels during the acute phase of infection are also associated with persistent infection (127). However, some patients without known immune deficiencies also exhibit persistent infections (128). Although a study suggest that persistent infection should be distinguished from long COVID (129), chronic symptoms such as cough and dyspnoea in persistent infection patients (130) may overlap with those observed in long COVID patients. Moreover, patients with persistent infection often demonstrate migratory, typical COVID-19 pneumonia patterns on chest CT (124). Therefore, persistent infection may be a contributing factor to post-COVID-19 symptoms in some patients. Research found a significant association between persistent viral presence and long COVID symptoms, with patients exhibiting higher viral copy numbers being more likely to develop long-term symptoms (131). When patients, particularly those with weakened immune systems, exhibit persistent PCR positivity and recurring symptoms over extended periods, persistent infection should be considered a potential mechanism.

sec.title: Rehabilitation and treatment strategies
sec.p: The management of post-COVID-19 condition is still at an exploratory stage, with limited clinical experience and research data. The management of respiratory symptoms in post-COVID-19 patients primarily includes rehabilitation therapy and physiotherapy-based approaches for managing dyspnoea (30). Self-generated strategies for daily living are also an important way for post-COVID-19 patients to adapt to the long-term effects of the condition and improve their quality of life (132). The treatments discussed in this section primarily include vaccination, PR, and pharmacological treatments.

Vaccination represents an effective strategy for preventing long COVID. Evidence indicates that patients with severe infections or those requiring mechanical ventilation during hospitalization are more likely to develop pulmonary sequelae (23,31). Notably, vaccination has been demonstrated to significantly lower the likelihood of severe infection and mechanical ventilation (133). Furthermore, vaccination has been found to lower the risk of developing long COVID symptoms (134-136). Two-dose vaccination compared to no vaccination was associated with a 36% reduction in the risk of long COVID, and compared to one-dose vaccination, the risk was reduced by 40% (135). However, for patients already experiencing long-term symptoms, vaccination does not appear to significantly alleviate their condition (135). Although vaccines may not entirely prevent long COVID, early vaccination plays a critical role in mitigating its long-term impact.

PR is a key component in the long-term management of chronic respiratory diseases, such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (137,138). PR involves a comprehensive assessment of the patient, followed by personalized interventions that include, but are not limited to, exercise training, patient education, and behavioral modifications (139). The manifestations of post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae are similar to those of chronic respiratory diseases, including dyspnoea, reduced exercise capacity, and abnormal lung function (14,30). Studies have demonstrated that PR can enhance exercise capacity, alleviate persistent symptoms, and improve the quality of life in patients with long COVID, although its effects on symptoms like dyspnoea and fatigue vary across studies (140-142). Therefore, further research is needed to optimize specific PR protocols and develop personalized rehabilitation plans for long COVID patients, thereby improving their quality of life more effectively.

The development of pharmacological interventions for long COVID is progressing at a relatively slow pace due to the complexity and heterogeneity of the disease, which pose significant challenges to research (143). Targeted interventions addressing specific phenotypes, such as respiratory sequelae, have the potential to improve patients’ quality of life. Based on the mechanisms underlying post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae discussed above, pharmacological treatments primarily include antifibrotic, anti-inflammatory, and anticoagulant therapies. Pirfenidone and nintedanib are currently regarded as potential therapeutic options for preventing post-COVID-19 pulmonary fibrosis (144-146), but their efficacy requires long-term evaluation. Corticosteroids are clinically used as potent anti-inflammatory agents for the treatment of autoimmune diseases and chronic inflammation (147). Evidence suggests that corticosteroid treatment can enhance lung function and alleviate symptoms such as dyspnoea in patients with persistent chest CT abnormalities (148,149). The improvement was similar between high-dose prednisolone (40 mg/day for 1 week, followed by a gradual reduction: 30 mg/day for 1 week, 20 mg/day for 2 weeks, and 10 mg/day for 2 weeks) and low-dose prednisolone (10 mg/day for 6 weeks), with no significant difference in efficacy (148). Additionally, N-acetylcysteine and suboxide have demonstrated potential in mitigating post-COVID-19 inflammation and endothelial dysfunction by modulating inflammatory and fibrinolytic factors (150). Future research could investigate other targeted anti-inflammatory therapies, including JAK inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, and TNF-α inhibitors, which target general inflammatory pathways or COVID-19-specific mechanisms (151). For COVID-19 patients discharged with a high risk of thrombosis, extended rivaroxaban thromboprophylaxis has been shown to significantly improve clinical outcomes (152). Antiplatelet agents (e.g., aspirin and clopidogrel) and anticoagulants (e.g., apixaban) may also have potential therapeutic applications in the management of patients with long COVID (153), although further research is required to validate their efficacy and safety. Additionally, antiviral drugs such as Paxlovid may play a significant role in managing long COVID (154). Preliminary evidence suggests that Paxlovid reduces the risk of developing long COVID and alleviates pulmonary burden (155).

For some patients with severe, irreversible COVID-19-related lung damage, such as those with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) unresponsive to mechanical ventilation and severe pulmonary fibrosis, lung transplantation may be the only viable treatment option (156,157). A study reported that patients who undergo bilateral orthotopic lung transplantation due to COVID-19 show significant improvements in activities of daily life and functional mobility after receiving pre- and post-transplant rehabilitation (158). However, lung transplantation should adhere to strict eligibility criteria, and patients must undergo comprehensive and rigorous evaluation before receiving lung transplantation (156).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: COVID-19 infection and its sequelae have significantly impacted both human health and socioeconomics. Post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae are prevalent, with clinical manifestations including dyspnoea, cough, hypoxia, and reduced exercise capacity. Among pulmonary function abnormalities, lung diffusion impairment is the most reported, and common residual abnormalities on chest CT include GGO and fibrotic-like changes. Notably, some patients continue to exhibit pulmonary sequelae even 3 years after discharge. The trajectory of long-term pulmonary recovery and the effects of different viral variants require further investigation through longitudinal follow-up studies.

Furthermore, this review presents an overview of the potential mechanisms and therapeutic strategies for post-COVID-19 pulmonary sequelae. A comprehensive understanding of the underlying mechanisms is critical for the development of more effective interventions. Future research should focus on comprehensive investigations into pathological mechanisms and the development of personalized therapeutic strategies, aiming to optimize clinical management, enhance patient quality of life, and alleviate the long-term health burden associated with post-COVID-19 conditions.
